items.title: XOR with a linear kernel fails, RBF succeeds
items.scenario: A toy 2D dataset has four points: (0,0) and (1,1) labeled class A; (0,1) and (1,0) labeled class B — the classic XOR pattern. A linear SVM (linear kernel) cannot separate the classes with a straight line. An RBF-kernel SVM with moderate γ draws a curved boundary that classifies all four points correctly.
items.explanation: The original coordinates are not linearly separable; RBF's implicit feature map makes the classes separable by a hyperplane in φ-space.

items.title: Polynomial kernel for mild curvature on tabular data
items.scenario: A fraud model uses three numeric features (transaction amount, hour, merchant risk score). A linear SVM plateaus at 0.82 AUC. Switching to a degree-2 polynomial kernel (γ=scale, r=1) captures interactions like high amount × risky merchant without manually engineering cross-terms.
items.explanation: Polynomial K encodes feature products up to degree d implicitly — useful when interactions matter but explicit expansion is tedious.

items.title: Text classification with linear kernel on TF-IDF
items.scenario: Spam detection represents each email as a 10,000-dimensional sparse TF-IDF vector. A linear kernel SVM trains in minutes and performs well because the high-dimensional bag-of-words space is already rich; adding RBF often adds little and slows training.
items.explanation: More complex kernels are not always better — when φ is already expressive (sparse high-d text), linear K(x,x′)=x·x′ may suffice.

items.title: RBF γ too large — memorizing noise
items.scenario: On 200 labeled medical records, an SVM with RBF and γ=100 fits wiggly islands around individual training points: training accuracy 100%, validation accuracy 62%. Reducing γ to 0.1 smooths the boundary; validation rises to 78%.
items.explanation: Large γ makes K(x,x′) spike only near training points — analogous to overfitting. Kernel hyperparameters need validation tuning.

items.title: Kernel PCA for nonlinear structure
items.scenario: Swiss-roll 3D points lie on a curved manifold. Linear PCA fails to unfold it. Kernel PCA with an RBF kernel computes principal directions in implicit φ-space and yields a 2D embedding that separates the roll layers more cleanly for visualization.
items.explanation: The kernel trick applies beyond SVM — any method expressed with dot products can use K for nonlinear geometry.
